does: This paper presents an indirect jailbreaking attack against large language models by leveraging the greater vulnerability of multimodal language models. The method first constructs an MLLM wrapper around a target LLM, then performs jailbreak attacks on the MLLM to obtain adversarial embeddings, which are subsequently converted back into textual suffixes that successfully compromise the original LLM. The approach incorporates an image-text semantic matching scheme to select optimal initial inputs that improve attack success rates. Experiments demonstrate the method achieves higher success rates and computational efficiency compared to direct LLM-jailbreak baselines, while exhibiting strong cross-class generalization across different types of harmful queries.
connects: This work extends the literature on adversarial attacks against language models by building on previous findings that multimodal systems present distinct vulnerabilities compared to text-only models. It responds to recent research on LLM jailbreaking, including suffix-based attacks and prompt injection techniques, by demonstrating that the multimodal pathway provides a more efficient attack surface. The paper also connects to broader work on the robustness and alignment of large neural models, particularly the observation that adding modalities can introduce new security gaps. The semantic matching component relates to prior work on input-output optimization and adversarial example construction in vision-language models, combining these threads to exploit the compositional nature of multimodal systems.
enables: This research highlights a critical vulnerability in the growing ecosystem of multimodal language models and suggests future work should prioritize defending multimodal systems as a necessary precursor to protecting their underlying text-based components. The indirect jailbreaking pathway opens questions about whether defenses that succeed on text-only models remain effective when auxiliary modalities are introduced, pointing toward new evaluation frameworks for LLM safety. The work enables development of stronger alignment techniques that account for multimodal attack vectors and suggests that robust LLM deployment may require careful architectural choices about how vision and language components interact. Additionally, the efficient attack methodology could inform better red-teaming practices and adversarial testing protocols for evaluating real-world LLM systems before deployment.
